item.title: Color-Color Selection of Quiescent Galaxies
item.description:
Star-formation in massive galaxies in the early universe was largely obscured by dust. This has the effect of reddening galaxy colors, making actively star-forming and passive, quiescent galaxies difficult to distinguish. In this project, I use the SIMBA cosmological simulations and POWDERDAY dust radiative transfer code to study a common method for classifying high-redshift galaxies: color-color selection on the UVJ diagram.

This work was conducted at the University of Florida as part of the REU program in computational and data-intensive astrophysics. I presented on this project at the 237th meeting of the American Astronomical Society, and my interavtive iPoster can be found here.

item.title: Automated Data Reduction
item.description: In the summer of 2018, I worked as a Research Assistant at the Guilford College Cline Observatory, studying variable stars and constructing a system for automatic data analysis and collection. The project was intended primarily to facilitate future student research using the tools I developed. The data analysis pipeline is detailed in the paper below.

item.title: Lucky Imaging
item.description: For my final project for an observational astronomy class at Guilford College, I used a method called Lucky Imaging to photograph planets and incorporate them into artistic photographs. The project was exhibited as a poster presentation at the Guilford Undergraduate Symposium, and turned into an article which was published in the October 2018 edition of The Physics Teacher.
